one_line_takeaway: A policy-grounded, 8-domain, 100k+-instance guardrail benchmark whose evaluation of 19 moderation models exposes systematic domain blind spots, non-monotonic gains from scale/evolution, a conservative false-negative bias, and near-total fragility to optimized adversarial suffixes for most models except WildGuard.
key_finding: Across 8 domains, guardrail models are domain-specialized and highly uneven: e.g., Granite Guardian (3B) leads in structured domains (HR/Finance/Education) while LLM Guard leads on informal Social Media. Series evolution broadens risk coverage but can regress common categories (LlamaGuard avg F1 0.294->0.605 across v1->v4, but Hate Speech peaks at LlamaGuard 3 = 0.777 and drops to 0.734 in v4). Scale does not guarantee quality: LlamaGuard 3 (1B) beats 8B on average F1 (0.485 vs 0.423) and Granite Guardian (3B) beats 5B (0.774 vs 0.749). Most models collapse under optimized attacks: average ASR exceeds 90% for Granite Guardian (5B) (0.928) and >60% for Aegis Defensive (0.677) and LLM Guard (0.645), while WildGuard is by far the most robust (0.198). Models show a conservative bias (Social Media avg precision 0.701 vs recall 0.479) and large per-category variance (Instagram Hate Speech avg F1 0.715 vs Identity Misrepresentation 0.273).
core_contribution: POLY-GUARD (the arXiv PDF's actual title; the feed record labeled it 'Guardset-x'), presented as the first massive, multi-domain, policy-grounded guardrail dataset for evaluating LLM content-moderation guardrail models. Its own novelty is a construction pipeline that scrapes 150+ real-world safety policy documents, extracts a two-level hierarchy of 400+ risk categories and 1,000+ granular safety rules across 8 safety-critical domains (social media, HR, finance, law, education, cybersecurity, code generation, general regulation), then generates 100k+ balanced safe/unsafe instances via rule-conditioned prompting of uncensored LLMs plus detoxification prompting for hard-benign counterparts. It further augments data with three interaction formats (statements, questions/instructions, multi-turn conversations) and attack-enhanced instances. The paper's contribution is the benchmark itself plus a large empirical study of 19 guardrail models; it does not propose a new guardrail model.
threat_model: The benchmark targets LLM content-safety guardrails (external moderation modules) as the system under test. Adversary: malicious users who submit policy-violating content or append optimized adversarial suffixes to evade moderation. Attack surface modeled: (1) domain- and rule-specific unsafe requests/statements/conversations; (2) hard-benign inputs that reference sensitive concepts to induce over-refusal (false positives); (3) attack-enhanced evasions using risk-category shifting, reasoning distraction, and instruction hijacking as seeds, then refined with adversarial prompt-optimization algorithms PAIR and AutoDAN to append suffixes that flip an originally-blocked unsafe example to 'safe'. Robustness is measured as attack success rate (ASR) on a filtered subset that all five top models correctly flagged pre-attack.
limitations: Authors' stated limitation: coverage skews to Western institutions and global platforms, lacking culturally diverse and region-specific (non-Western) safety risks. Additional reviewer-noted caveats grounded in the text: unsafe/safe examples are LLM-generated (uncensored LLMs + GPT-4o for rule extraction and detoxification), so label quality and realism inherit generator biases; the paper reports no human-validation numbers in the fetched pages for the 100k+ synthetic instances; ASR is measured only on the five most advanced models and on a filtered all-correct subset, so it is not directly comparable across the full model set; metric choice excludes AUPRC by design, so ranking relies entirely on discrete outputs at default thresholds.

datasets_used: POLY-GUARD (this paper's own dataset): 150+ safety policies, 400+ risk categories, 1,000+ safety rules, 100k+ instances across 8 domains (social media, HR, finance, law, education, cybersecurity, code generation, general regulation)

Policy sources cited as grounding: EU AI Act, GDPR, FINRA 2025 oversight report, OECD AI-in-Finance framework, U.S. Treasury 2024 AI-in-financial-services review, Alan Turing Institute, Financial Stability Institute, American Bar Association, US/state bar associations, OpenAI usage policy, CWE, OWASP, MITRE, NIST, CVE, UNESCO
PAIR and AutoDAN used as adversarial prompt-optimization algorithms to craft attack-enhanced instances
quantitative_results: Scale: 150+ policies, 400+ risk categories, 1000+ safety rules, 100k+ instances, 8 domains, 19 guardrail models evaluated

LlamaGuard series evolution on Instagram (avg F1): 0.294 (v1) -> 0.605 (v4); Cybersecurity 0.472->0.797; Platform Abuse 0.151->0.440; Misinformation 0.045->0.692; Hate Speech peaks at LlamaGuard 3 (0.777), drops to 0.734 in LlamaGuard 4
Scaling comparison (avg F1): LlamaGuard 3 1B = 0.485 > 8B = 0.423; Granite Guardian 3B = 0.774 > 5B = 0.749
Adversarial ASR (Table 2, average across domains): Granite Guardian (5B) 0.928 (highest); MDJudge 2 0.776; Aegis Defensive 0.677; LLM Guard 0.645; WildGuard 0.198 (most robust)
Contextual moderation: 12/14 models on Code and 13/14 on Cyber score higher F1 on conversational vs statement/instruction instances, avg improvement >5% F1
Conservative bias: Social Media avg precision 0.701 vs avg recall 0.479
Category-skew: Instagram Hate Speech avg F1 0.715 vs Identity Misrepresentation 0.273; avg per-category F1 std dev generally >10%

Table 1 selected F1/Recall (x100) highlights: Granite Guardian 3B Law 90.4/86.0, Cyber 85.0/90.0; WildGuard Social-Media Messaging 76.0/85.1; MDJudge 2 Cyber 89.1/90.1; ShieldGemma 2B and TextMod API near 0 on several domains (e.g., EU AI Act 0.00/0.00)
baselines_compared: Meta: LlamaGuard 1, LlamaGuard 2, LlamaGuard 3 (1B), LlamaGuard 3 (8B), LlamaGuard 4
